title: Willie Nelson

infobox.value: Willie Hugh Nelson   (1933-04-29) April 29, 1933 (age 92)  [Abbott, Texas](/wiki/Abbott,_Texas "Abbott, Texas"), U.S.

* Martha Matthews  ​  ​ (m. 1952; div. 1962)​ * [Shirley Collie](/wiki/Shirley_Collie_Nelson "Shirley Collie Nelson")  ​  ​ (m. 1963; div. 1971)​ * Connie Koepke  ​  ​ (m. 1971; div. 1988)​ * Annie D'Angelo  ​ (m. 1991)​

* [Albums](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_albums_discography "Willie Nelson albums discography") * [singles](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_singles_discography "Willie Nelson singles discography") * [songwriting](/wiki/List_of_songs_written_by_Willie_Nelson "List of songs written by Willie Nelson")

* [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_Records "Liberty Records") * [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") * [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") * [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") * [Island](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records") * Justice Records * [Lost Highway](/wiki/Lost_Highway_Records "Lost Highway Records") * [Legacy Recordings](/wiki/Legacy_Recordings "Legacy Recordings")
[United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force")

(born April 29, 1933) is an American singer, guitarist, songwriter, actor, and activist. He was one of the main figures of the [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") subgenre that developed in the late 1960s as a reaction to the conservative restrictions of the [Nashville sound](/wiki/Nashville_sound "Nashville sound"). The critical success of his album *[Shotgun Willie](/wiki/Shotgun_Willie "Shotgun Willie")* (1973), combined with the critical and commercial success of *[Red Headed Stranger](/wiki/Red_Headed_Stranger "Red Headed Stranger")* (1975) and *[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Stardust (Willie Nelson album)")* (1978), made Nelson one of the most recognized artists in country music. Nelson has acted in over 30 films, co-authored several books, and has been involved in activism for the use of [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") and the [legalization of marijuana](/wiki/Legalization_of_non-medical_cannabis_in_the_United_States "Legalization of non-medical cannabis in the United States").

American country musician (born 1933)

For the American boxer, see [Willie Nelson (boxer)](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_(boxer) "Willie Nelson (boxer)"). For the Scottish rugby player, see [Willie Neilson](/wiki/Willie_Neilson "Willie Neilson"). **Willie Hugh Nelson** (born April 29, 1933) is an American singer, guitarist, songwriter, actor, and activist. He was one of the main figures of the [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country") subgenre that developed in the late 1960s as a reaction to the conservative restrictions of the [Nashville sound](/wiki/Nashville_sound "Nashville sound"). The critical success of his album *[Shotgun Willie](/wiki/Shotgun_Willie "Shotgun Willie")* (1973), combined with the critical and commercial success of *[Red Headed Stranger](/wiki/Red_Headed_Stranger "Red Headed Stranger")* (1975) and *[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Stardust (Willie Nelson album)")* (1978), made Nelson one of the most recognized artists in country music. Nelson has acted in over 30 films, co-authored several books, and has been involved in activism for the use of [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") and the [legalization of marijuana](/wiki/Legalization_of_non-medical_cannabis_in_the_United_States "Legalization of non-medical cannabis in the United States").

Nelson wrote his first song at the age of seven and joined his first band at ten. During high school, he toured locally with the Bohemian Polka as their lead singer and guitar player. After graduating from high school in 1950, he joined the [U.S. Air Force](/wiki/U.S._Air_Force "U.S. Air Force") but was later discharged due to back problems. After his return, Nelson attended [Baylor University](/wiki/Baylor_University "Baylor University") for two years but dropped out because he was succeeding in music. He worked as a [disc jockey](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") at radio stations in his native Texas, and at several radio stations in the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest"), all the while working as a singer and songwriter throughout the late 1950s. During that time, he wrote songs that would become country standards, including "[Funny How Time Slips Away](/wiki/Funny_How_Time_Slips_Away "Funny How Time Slips Away")", "[Hello Walls](/wiki/Hello_Walls "Hello Walls")", "[Pretty Paper](/wiki/Pretty_Paper_(song) "Pretty Paper (song)")", and "[Crazy](/wiki/Crazy_(Willie_Nelson_song) "Crazy (Willie Nelson song)")".

In 1960, Nelson moved to [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee"), then signed a publishing contract with Pamper Music that allowed him to join [Ray Price](/wiki/Ray_Price "Ray Price")'s band as a bassist. In 1962, he recorded his first album, *[...And Then I Wrote](/wiki/...And_Then_I_Wrote "...And Then I Wrote")*. Its success enabled Nelson to sign with [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") in 1964 and join the [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") the following year. After mid-chart hits in the late 1960s and early 1970s, Nelson grew weary of the corporate Nashville music scene. In 1972, he moved to [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), returned to performing, and appeared frequently at the [Armadillo World Headquarters](/wiki/Armadillo_World_Headquarters "Armadillo World Headquarters").

In 1973, after signing with [Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records"), Nelson turned to outlaw country, including albums such as *Shotgun Willie* and *Phases and Stages*. In 1975, he switched to Columbia Records, where he recorded the critically acclaimed album *Red Headed Stranger*. The same year, he recorded another outlaw country album, *[Wanted! The Outlaws](/wiki/Wanted!_The_Outlaws "Wanted! The Outlaws")*, with [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings"), [Jessi Colter](/wiki/Jessi_Colter "Jessi Colter"), and [Tompall Glaser](/wiki/Tompall_Glaser "Tompall Glaser"). During the mid-1980s, while creating hit albums such as *[Honeysuckle Rose](/wiki/Honeysuckle_Rose_(album) "Honeysuckle Rose (album)")* and recording hit songs such as "[On the Road Again](/wiki/On_the_Road_Again_(Willie_Nelson_song) "On the Road Again (Willie Nelson song)")", "[To All the Girls I've Loved Before](/wiki/To_All_the_Girls_I%27ve_Loved_Before "To All the Girls I've Loved Before")", and "[Pancho and Lefty](/wiki/Pancho_and_Lefty "Pancho and Lefty")", he joined the country [supergroup](/wiki/Supergroup_(music) "Supergroup (music)") [The Highwaymen](/wiki/The_Highwaymen_(country_supergroup) "The Highwaymen (country supergroup)"), along with fellow singers Jennings, [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), and [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson"). In 1985, he helped organize the first [Farm Aid](/wiki/Farm_Aid "Farm Aid") concert to benefit American farmers; he has appeared at the annual concerts ever since.

In 1990, Nelson's assets were seized by the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service"), which claimed that he owed $32 million. The difficulty of paying his outstanding debt was aggravated by weak investments he had made during the 1980s. In 1992, Nelson released *[The IRS Tapes: Who'll Buy My Memories?](/wiki/The_IRS_Tapes:_Who%27ll_Buy_My_Memories%3F "The IRS Tapes: Who'll Buy My Memories?")*; the profits of the double album—destined to the IRS—and the auction of Nelson's assets cleared his debt. During the 1990s and 2000s, Nelson continued touring extensively and released albums every year. Reviews ranged from positive to mixed. He explored genres such as reggae, blues, jazz, and folk.

Nelson made his first movie appearance in the 1979 film *[The Electric Horseman](/wiki/The_Electric_Horseman "The Electric Horseman")*, followed by other appearances in movies and on television. Nelson is a major [liberal](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States") activist and the co-chair of the advisory board of the [National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws](/wiki/National_Organization_for_the_Reform_of_Marijuana_Laws "National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws") (NORML), which is in favor of marijuana legalization. On the environmental front, Nelson owns the [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") brand [Willie Nelson Biodiesel](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_Biodiesel "Willie Nelson Biodiesel"), whose product is made from vegetable oil. Nelson is also the honorary chairman of the advisory board of the Texas Music Project, the official music charity of the state of Texas.

## Early life and education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Young-willie-nelson.jpg/120px-Young-willie-nelson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young-willie-nelson.jpg)

[![=A man with a leather helmet and a white football jersey](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Willie-Nelson-Highschool.jpg/120px-Willie-Nelson-Highschool.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie-Nelson-Highschool.jpg)

Nelson as a junior at [Abbott High School](/wiki/Abbott_High_School "Abbott High School") (top, pictured in 1949). Nelson's high school football portrait, (bottom, c. 1950)

Willie Hugh Nelson was born in [Abbott, Texas](/wiki/Abbott,_Texas "Abbott, Texas"), on April 29, 1933,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski200813-1) to Ira Doyle Nelson and Myrle Marie (*née* Greenhaw).[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobey198258-2) His birthday was incorrectly recorded by Dr. F. D. Sims as April 30.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski200813-1) He was named Willie by his cousin Mildred, who also chose Hugh as his middle name, in honor of her recently deceased younger brother.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski200813-1) Nelson traces his genealogy to the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), in which his ancestor John Nelson served as a major.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunt2009-3) His parents moved to Texas from [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") in 1929 to look for work. His grandfather, William, worked as a blacksmith, while his father worked as a mechanic.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2007a29-4) His mother left soon after he was born,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaufenberg2005473-5) and his father remarried and also moved away, leaving Nelson and his sister [Bobbie](/wiki/Bobbie_Nelson "Bobbie Nelson") to be raised by their grandparents, who taught singing back in Arkansas and started their grandchildren in music.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski20086-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2004[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidqCDF5fFuBT8CpgPA218_218]-7) Nelson's grandfather bought him a guitar when he was six, and taught him a few [chords](/wiki/Guitar_chord "Guitar chord"),[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2007a29-4) and Nelson sang gospel songs in the local church alongside Bobbie.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalone2002303-8) He wrote his first song at age seven,[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008-9) and when he was nine, he played guitar for local band Bohemian Polka.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKienzle2003236-10)

During the summer, the Nelson family [picked cotton](/wiki/Cotton_picking "Cotton picking") alongside other Abbott residents.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond2000[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidZqZ4PgAACAAJpgPA7_7,_8,_23]-11) Nelson disliked picking cotton, so he earned money by singing in dance halls, taverns, and [honky tonks](/wiki/Honky-tonk "Honky-tonk") from age 13, which he continued through high school.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobey198247-12) His musical influences were [Jimmie Rodgers](/wiki/Jimmie_Rodgers "Jimmie Rodgers"), [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams"), [Bob Wills](/wiki/Bob_Wills "Bob Wills"), [Lefty Frizzell](/wiki/Lefty_Frizzell "Lefty Frizzell"), [Ray Price](/wiki/Ray_Price "Ray Price"), [Ernest Tubb](/wiki/Ernest_Tubb "Ernest Tubb"), [Hank Snow](/wiki/Hank_Snow "Hank Snow"), [Django Reinhardt](/wiki/Django_Reinhardt "Django Reinhardt"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), and [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond2000[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidZqZ4PgAACAAJpgPA17_17]-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHann2012-15)

Nelson attended [Abbott High School](/wiki/Abbott_High_School "Abbott High School"), where he was a halfback on the [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team, guard on the [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team, and [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") in [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"). He also raised [pigs](/wiki/Pig "Pig") with the [Future Farmers of America](/wiki/National_FFA_Organization "National FFA Organization").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobey198258-2) While still at school, he sang and played guitar in The Texans, a band formed by his sister's husband, Bud Fletcher.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKienzle2003236-10) The band played in [honky-tonks](/wiki/Honky-tonk "Honky-tonk"), and also had a Sunday morning spot at [KHBR](/wiki/KHBR_(AM) "KHBR (AM)") in [Hillsboro, Texas](/wiki/Hillsboro,_Texas "Hillsboro, Texas"). Meanwhile, Nelson had a short stint as a relief phone operator in Abbott, followed by a job as a [tree trimmer](/wiki/Tree_care "Tree care") for the local electric company, as well as a [pawn shop](/wiki/Pawn_shop "Pawn shop") employee.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19694-16) After leaving school in 1950, he joined the [U.S. Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"); he served for eight months before being medically discharged because of issues with his back.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChapman2010392-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomson201230-18) Upon his return, he married Martha Matthews in 1952, and from 1954 to 1956 studied agriculture at [Baylor University](/wiki/Baylor_University "Baylor University"), where he joined the [Tau Kappa Epsilon](/wiki/Tau_Kappa_Epsilon "Tau Kappa Epsilon") fraternity, until dropping out to pursue a career in music.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond200024-19) He worked as a nightclub bouncer, autohouse partsman, saddle maker, and tree trimmer again. He later joined [Johnny Bush](/wiki/Johnny_Bush "Johnny Bush")'s band.

Nelson moved with his family to [Pleasanton, Texas](/wiki/Pleasanton,_Texas "Pleasanton, Texas"), where he auditioned for a job as a DJ at [KBOP](/wiki/KWMF "KWMF"). The owner of the station, Dr. Ben Parker, gave Nelson the job despite his lack of experience working on radio.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19694-16) With the equipment of the station, Nelson made his first two recordings in 1955: "[The Storm Has Just Begun](/wiki/The_Storm_Has_Just_Begun "The Storm Has Just Begun")" and "When I've Sung My Last Hillbilly Song". He recorded the tracks on used tapes, and sent the demos to the local label SARG Records, which rejected them.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomson201224-20) He then had stints working for KDNT in [Denton](/wiki/Denton,_Texas "Denton, Texas"), [KCUL](/wiki/KAMM_(AM) "KAMM (AM)"), and [KCNC](/wiki/KFJZ "KFJZ") in [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas"), where he hosted *[The Western Express](/wiki/The_Western_Express "The Western Express")*, taught Sunday school, and played in nightclubs. He then decided to move to [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") but, when he was unable to find a job there, he hitchhiked to [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), where his mother lived.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19694-16) When nobody picked him up, he ended up sleeping in a ditch[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz201592-21) before hopping a [freight train](/wiki/Rail_freight_transport "Rail freight transport") bound for [Eugene](/wiki/Eugene,_Oregon "Eugene, Oregon"). A truck driver drove him to a bus station and loaned him $10 for a ticket to reach [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz201593-22)

## Music career

### Beginnings (1956–1971)

Nelson was hired by [KVAN](/wiki/KVAN_(AM) "KVAN (AM)") in [Vancouver, Washington](/wiki/Vancouver,_Washington "Vancouver, Washington"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) and appeared frequently on a television show.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19694-16)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski200874–76-24) He made his first record in 1956, "[No Place for Me](/wiki/No_Place_for_Me "No Place for Me")", that included [Leon Payne](/wiki/Leon_Payne "Leon Payne")'s "Lumberjack" on the B-side.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200670-25) The recording failed.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicair2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidMYMQl9dsKJECpgPA246_246]-26) Nelson continued working as a radio announcer and singing in Vancouver clubs.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine1997324-27)
He made several appearances in a Colorado nightclub, later moving to [Springfield, Missouri](/wiki/Springfield,_Missouri "Springfield, Missouri"). After failing to land a spot on the *[Ozark Jubilee](/wiki/Ozark_Jubilee "Ozark Jubilee")*, he started to work as a dishwasher. Unhappy with his job, he moved back to Texas. After a short time in [Waco](/wiki/Waco,_Texas "Waco, Texas"), he settled in Fort Worth, and quit the music business for a year.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19694-16) He sold Bibles and vacuum cleaners door-to-door,[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDingus1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid77pgPA77_77]-28) and eventually became a sales manager for the *[Encyclopedia Americana](/wiki/Encyclopedia_Americana "Encyclopedia Americana")*.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19695-29)

After his son Billy was born in 1958, the family moved to [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), Texas. On the way, Nelson stopped by the [Esquire Ballroom](/wiki/Esquire_Ballroom "Esquire Ballroom") to sell his original songs to house band singer [Larry Butler](/wiki/Larry_Butler_(producer) "Larry Butler (producer)"). Butler refused to purchase the song "[Mr. Record Man](/wiki/Mr._Record_Man "Mr. Record Man")" for $10, instead giving Nelson a $50 loan to rent an apartment and a six-night job singing in the club.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000116,_117-30) Nelson rented the apartment near Houston in [Pasadena, Texas](/wiki/Pasadena,_Texas "Pasadena, Texas"), where he also worked at the radio station as the sign-on disc jockey. During this time, he recorded two singles for [Pappy Daily](/wiki/Pappy_Daily "Pappy Daily") on [D Records](/wiki/D_Records "D Records")[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2013-31) "[Man With the Blues](/wiki/Man_With_the_Blues "Man With the Blues")"/"The Storm Has Just Begun" and "[What a Way to Live](/wiki/What_a_Way_to_Live_(song) "What a Way to Live (song)")"/"Misery Mansion".[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000117-32) Nelson then was hired by guitar instructor Paul Buskirk to work as an instructor in his school. He sold "[Family Bible](/wiki/Family_Bible_(song) "Family Bible (song)")" to Buskirk for $50 and "[Night Life](/wiki/Night_Life_(Willie_Nelson_song) "Night Life (Willie Nelson song)")" for $150.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000118-33) "Family Bible" turned into a hit for [Claude Gray](/wiki/Claude_Gray "Claude Gray") in 1960.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008102-34)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Hello Walls"](/wiki/File:Hello_Walls.ogg "File:Hello Walls.ogg")

Written by Willie Nelson, "Hello Walls", was a hit for Faron Young in 1961, and the song that gave Nelson national recognition as a songwriter. He recorded the song for his debut album *... And Then I Wrote*.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Nelson moved to [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee"), in 1960, but was unable to find a label to sign him. During this period he often spent time at [Tootsie's Orchid Lounge](/wiki/Tootsie%27s_Orchid_Lounge "Tootsie's Orchid Lounge"), a bar near the Grand Ole Opry frequented by the show's stars and other singers and songwriters.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond200036-35) There Nelson met [Hank Cochran](/wiki/Hank_Cochran "Hank Cochran"), a songwriter who worked for the publishing company Pamper Music, owned by Ray Price and Hal Smith. Cochran heard Nelson during a jam session with [Buddy Emmons](/wiki/Buddy_Emmons "Buddy Emmons") and Jimmy Day. Cochran had just earned a raise of $50 a week, but convinced Smith to pay Nelson the money instead to sign him to Pamper Music. On hearing Nelson sing "Hello Walls" at Tootsie's, [Faron Young](/wiki/Faron_Young "Faron Young") decided to record it.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKosser2006[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidDL6gHNXWToQCpgPA73_73]-36) After Ray Price recorded Nelson's "Night Life", and his previous bassist [Johnny Paycheck](/wiki/Johnny_Paycheck "Johnny Paycheck") quit, Nelson joined Price's touring band as a bass player. While playing with Price and the [Cherokee Cowboys](/w/index.php?title=Cherokee_Cowboys&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cherokee Cowboys (page does not exist)"), his songs became hits for other artists, including "[Funny How Time Slips Away](/wiki/Funny_How_Time_Slips_Away "Funny How Time Slips Away")" ([Billy Walker](/wiki/Billy_Walker_(singer) "Billy Walker (singer)")), "[Pretty Paper](/wiki/Pretty_Paper_(song) "Pretty Paper (song)")" ([Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison")), and, most famously, "[Crazy](/wiki/Crazy_(Willie_Nelson_song) "Crazy (Willie Nelson song)")" by [Patsy Cline](/wiki/Patsy_Cline "Patsy Cline").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine1997324-27) Nelson and Cochran also met Cline's husband, [Charlie Dick](/wiki/Charlie_Dick "Charlie Dick") at Tootsie's. Dick liked a song of Nelson's he heard on the bar's jukebox. Nelson played him a demo tape of "Crazy". Later that night Dick played the tape for Cline, who decided to record it.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski20086-6) "Crazy" became the biggest [jukebox](/wiki/Jukebox "Jukebox") hit of all time.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENPR_staff1996-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Willie_Nelson_Grand_Ole_Opry_publicity_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Willie_Nelson_Grand_Ole_Opry_publicity_-_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Nelson_Grand_Ole_Opry_publicity_-_cropped.jpg)

Nelson performing on a [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") package show in 1965

Nelson signed with [Liberty Records](/wiki/Liberty_Records "Liberty Records") and was recording by August 1961 at the [Bradley Studios](/wiki/Bradley_Studios "Bradley Studios") in Nashville. His first two successful singles as an artist were released by the next year, including "[Willingly](/wiki/Willingly "Willingly")" (a duet with his soon-to-be second wife, [Shirley Collie](/wiki/Shirley_Collie_Nelson "Shirley Collie Nelson"), which became his first charting single and first Top Ten at No. 10) and "[Touch Me](/wiki/Touch_Me_(Willie_Nelson_song) "Touch Me (Willie Nelson song)")" (his second Top Ten, stalling at No. 7).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwardsCallahan2001-38) Nelson's tenure at Liberty yielded his first album entitled *[...And Then I Wrote](/wiki/...And_Then_I_Wrote "...And Then I Wrote")*, released in September 1962.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBushMitchell200779-39) In 1963 Collie and Nelson were married in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"). He then worked on the west coast offices of Pamper Records, in [Pico Rivera, California](/wiki/Pico_Rivera,_California "Pico Rivera, California"). Since the job did not allow him the time to play music of his own, he left it and bought a ranch in [Ridgetop, Tennessee](/wiki/Ridgetop,_Tennessee "Ridgetop, Tennessee"), outside of Nashville.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMyers19695-29) [Fred Foster](/wiki/Fred_Foster "Fred Foster") of [Monument Records](/wiki/Monument_Records "Monument Records") signed Nelson in early 1964, but only one single was released: "I Never Cared For You".[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobey1982190-40)

By the fall of 1964, Nelson had moved to [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") at the behest of [Chet Atkins](/wiki/Chet_Atkins "Chet Atkins"), signing a contract for $10,000 per year.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2004[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid224pgPA224_224]-41) *[Country Willie – His Own Songs](/wiki/Country_Willie_%E2%80%93_His_Own_Songs "Country Willie – His Own Songs")* became Nelson's first RCA Victor album, recorded in April 1965. That same year he joined the [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry"),[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008Nashville,_1960-42) and he met and became friends with [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings") after watching one of his shows in [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000158-43) In 1967, he formed his backing band "The Record Men", featuring Johnny Bush, [Jimmy Day](/wiki/Jimmy_Day "Jimmy Day"), [Paul English](/wiki/Paul_English_(drummer) "Paul English (drummer)") and David Zettner.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBushMitchell2007137–138-44) During his first few years on RCA Victor, Nelson had no significant hits, but from November 1966 through March 1969, his singles reached the Top 25 in a consistent manner. "One in a Row" (#19, 1966), "The Party's Over" (#24 during a 16-week chart run in 1967), and his cover of [Morecambe & Wise](/wiki/Morecambe_%26_Wise "Morecambe & Wise")'s "Bring Me Sunshine" (#13, March 1969) were Nelson's best-selling records during his time with RCA.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicair2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidMYMQl9dsKJECpgPA246_246]-26)

By 1970, most of Nelson's songwriting royalties were invested in tours that did not produce significant profits. In addition to the problems in his career, Nelson divorced Shirley Collie in 1970. In December, his ranch in Ridgetop, Tennessee, burned down. He interpreted the incident as a signal for a change. He moved to a ranch near [Bandera, Texas](/wiki/Bandera,_Texas "Bandera, Texas"), and married Connie Koepke. In early 1971 his single "I'm a Memory" reached the top 30.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKienzle2003[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidGq2TTa6TStkCpgPA248_248]-45) After he recorded his final RCA single, "Mountain Dew" (backed with "Phases, Stages, Circles, Cycles and Scenes"), in late April 1972, RCA requested that Nelson renew his contract ahead of schedule, with the implication that RCA would not release his latest recordings if he did not.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2004[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidqCDF5fFuBT8CpgPA223_223]-46) Due to the failure of his albums, and particularly frustrated by the reception of *[Yesterday's Wine](/wiki/Yesterday%27s_Wine "Yesterday's Wine")*, although his contract was not over, Nelson decided to retire from music.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000167-47)

### Outlaw country and success (1972–1989)

Nelson moved to [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), where the burgeoning hippie music scene (see [Armadillo World Headquarters](/wiki/Armadillo_World_Headquarters "Armadillo World Headquarters")) rejuvenated the singer. His popularity in Austin soared as he played his own brand of country music marked by country, folk and jazz influences.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReidSahm2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid1gry61absiECpgPA79_79]-48) In March, he performed on the final day of the *Dripping Springs Reunion*, a three-day country music festival aimed by its producers to be an annual event. Despite the failure to reach the expected attendance, the concept of the festival inspired Nelson to create the *[Fourth of July Picnic](/wiki/Willie_Nelson%27s_Fourth_of_July_Picnic "Willie Nelson's Fourth of July Picnic")*, his own annual event, starting the following year.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2012-49)

Nelson decided to return to the recording business; he signed Neil Reshen as his manager to negotiate with RCA, who got the label to agree to end his contract upon repayment of $14,000.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2004[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidqCDF5fFuBT8CpgPA223_223]-46) Reshen eventually signed Nelson to [Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") for $25,000 per year, where he became the label's first country artist.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2004[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid224pgPA224-50) He formed his backing band, *[The Family](/wiki/Family_(Willie_Nelson%27s_band) "Family (Willie Nelson's band)")*,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilner1998[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidzeslYkOZIvUCpgPA183_183],_[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid184pgPA184_184]-51) and, by February 1973, he was recording his acclaimed *[Shotgun Willie](/wiki/Shotgun_Willie "Shotgun Willie")* at Atlantic Studios in New York City.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardenHoekstraMcCallMorris1996169-52)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Shotgun Willie introduction"](/wiki/File:Shotgun_Willie.ogg "File:Shotgun Willie.ogg")

Introduction of the song "Shotgun Willie", opening track of the album *[Shotgun Willie](/wiki/Shotgun_Willie "Shotgun Willie")*, that marked a change of style from Nelson's earlier recordings.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

*Shotgun Willie*, released in May 1973, earned excellent reviews but did not sell well. The album led Nelson to a new style, later stating that *Shotgun Willie* had "cleared his throat".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETichi1998341-53) His next release, *[Phases and Stages](/wiki/Phases_and_Stages "Phases and Stages")*, released in 1974, was a concept album about a couple's divorce, inspired by his own experience. Side one of the record is from the viewpoint of the woman, and side two is from the viewpoint of the man.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2007-54) The album included the hit single "[Bloody Mary Morning](/wiki/Bloody_Mary_Morning "Bloody Mary Morning")".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicair2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidMYMQl9dsKJECpgPA247_247]-55) The same year, he produced and starred in the pilot episode of [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")' *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")*.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond200075-56)

Nelson then moved to [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"), where he signed a contract that gave him complete creative control, made possible by the critical and commercial success of his previous albums.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicair2007[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid247pgPA247_247]-57) The result was the critically acclaimed and massively popular 1975 concept album *[Red Headed Stranger](/wiki/Red_Headed_Stranger "Red Headed Stranger")*. Although Columbia was reluctant to release an album with primarily a guitar and piano for accompaniment, Nelson and Waylon Jennings insisted. The album included a cover of [Fred Rose](/wiki/Fred_Rose_(songwriter) "Fred Rose (songwriter)")'s 1945 song "[Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain](/wiki/Blue_Eyes_Crying_in_the_Rain "Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain")", that had been released as a single previous to the album, and became Nelson's first number one hit as a singer.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolffDuane2000[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid3Jorozp1yp4CpgPA367_367]-58) Throughout his 1975 tour, Nelson raised funds for PBS-affiliated stations across the south promoting *Austin City Limits*. The pilot was aired first on those stations, later being released nationwide. The positive reception of the show prompted PBS to order ten episodes for 1976, formally launching the show.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond200076-59)

[![Three men. From left to right, the first man has brown hair and beard. He wears a blue T-shirt and a white jacket and is looking at the man in the middle. The man in the middle wears a green cap and shades, and long red hair. He wears a brown T-shirt. The man at the right has brown hair, he looks at the man at the middle. He wears a white shirt and a black letter jacket.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Kris_Willie_Waylon.jpg/250px-Kris_Willie_Waylon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kris_Willie_Waylon.jpg)

L–R: [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson"), Nelson, and [Waylon Jennings](/wiki/Waylon_Jennings "Waylon Jennings") at the 1972 *[Dripping Springs Reunion](/wiki/Dripping_Springs_Reunion "Dripping Springs Reunion")*

As Jennings was also achieving success in country music in the early 1970s, the pair were combined into a genre called [outlaw country](/wiki/Outlaw_country "Outlaw country"), since it did not conform to Nashville standards.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartman2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid174pgPA174_174]-60) The album *[Wanted! The Outlaws](/wiki/Wanted!_The_Outlaws "Wanted! The Outlaws")* in 1976 with [Jessi Colter](/wiki/Jessi_Colter "Jessi Colter") and [Tompall Glaser](/wiki/Tompall_Glaser "Tompall Glaser") cemented the pair's outlaw image and became country music's first [platinum album](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartman2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid175pgPA175_175]-61) Later that year Nelson released *[The Sound in Your Mind](/wiki/The_Sound_in_Your_Mind "The Sound in Your Mind")* (certified gold in 1978 and platinum in 2001)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERIAA_staff2010-62) and his first gospel album *[Troublemaker](/wiki/The_Troublemaker_(album) "The Troublemaker (album)")*[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2008-63) (certified gold in 1986).[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERIAA_staff_22010-64)

In the summer of 1977, Nelson discovered that Reshen had been filing tax extensions and not paying the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") (IRS) since he took over as his manager.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015263-65) In June, a package containing cocaine was sent from Reshen's office in New York to Jennings in Nashville.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015262-66) The package was followed by the [DEA](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration"), and Jennings was arrested. The charges were later dropped, since Reshen's assistant, Mark Rothbaum, stepped in and took the charges. Rothbaum was sentenced to serve time in jail. Impressed by his attitude, Nelson fired Reshen and hired Rothbaum as his manager.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015263-65) In 1978, Nelson released two more platinum albums. One, *[Waylon & Willie](/wiki/Waylon_%26_Willie "Waylon & Willie")*, was a collaboration with Jennings that included "[Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys](/wiki/Mammas_Don%27t_Let_Your_Babies_Grow_Up_to_Be_Cowboys "Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys")", a hit single written and performed by [Ed Bruce](/wiki/Ed_Bruce "Ed Bruce").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenningsKaye199610-67) Though observers predicted that *[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Stardust (Willie Nelson album)")* would ruin his career, it went platinum the same year.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERIAA_staff_32010-68) Nelson continued to top the charts with hit songs during the late 1970s, including "[Good Hearted Woman](/wiki/Good_Hearted_Woman_(song) "Good Hearted Woman (song)")", "Remember Me",[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillboard1976-69) "[If You've Got the Money I've Got the Time](/wiki/If_You%27ve_Got_the_Money_I%27ve_Got_the_Time "If You've Got the Money I've Got the Time")", and "[Uncloudy Day](/wiki/Uncloudy_Day "Uncloudy Day")".[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETribe2006188-70)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["On the Road Again"](/wiki/File:On_the_Road_Again.ogg "File:On the Road Again.ogg")

From the album *Honeysuckle Rose*, "On the Road Again" peaked at number one on the [Hot Country Songs](/wiki/Hot_Country_Songs "Hot Country Songs") chart in 1980.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

During the 1980s, Nelson recorded a series of hit singles including "[Midnight Rider](/wiki/Midnight_Rider "Midnight Rider")", a 1980 cover of the [Allman Brothers](/wiki/Allman_Brothers "Allman Brothers") song which Nelson recorded for *[The Electric Horseman](/wiki/The_Electric_Horseman "The Electric Horseman")*,[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarrison,_Thomas201196-71) the soundtrack "[On the Road Again](/wiki/On_the_Road_Again_(Willie_Nelson_song) "On the Road Again (Willie Nelson song)")" from the movie *[Honeysuckle Rose](/wiki/Honeysuckle_Rose_(film) "Honeysuckle Rose (film)")*, and a duet with [Julio Iglesias](/wiki/Julio_Iglesias "Julio Iglesias") titled "[To All the Girls I've Loved Before](/wiki/To_All_the_Girls_I%27ve_Loved_Before "To All the Girls I've Loved Before")".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJurek2008-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/President_Jimmy_Carter_with_Willie_Nelson_and_his_guests.jpg/250px-President_Jimmy_Carter_with_Willie_Nelson_and_his_guests.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Jimmy_Carter_with_Willie_Nelson_and_his_guests.jpg)

Nelson and guests with President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") in 1978

In 1982, *[Pancho & Lefty](/wiki/Pancho_%26_Lefty_(album) "Pancho & Lefty (album)")*, a duet album with [Merle Haggard](/wiki/Merle_Haggard "Merle Haggard") produced by [Chips Moman](/wiki/Chips_Moman "Chips Moman"), was released.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonkman2008-73) During the recording sessions of *Pancho & Lefty*, Johnny Christopher, a session guitarist and the co-writer of "[Always on My Mind](/wiki/Always_on_My_Mind "Always on My Mind")", tried to pitch the song to an uninterested Haggard. Nelson, who was unaware of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")'s version of the song, asked him to record it. Produced by Moman, the single of the song was released, as well as the album [*Always on My Mind*](/wiki/Always_on_My_Mind_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Always on My Mind (Willie Nelson album)"). The single topped *Billboard*'s Hot Country Singles, while it reached number five on the *Billboard* Hot 100. The release won three awards during the [25th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/25th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "25th Annual Grammy Awards"): [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year"), [Best Country Song](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Country_Song "Grammy Award for Best Country Song") and [Best Male Country Vocal Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Male_Country_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Male Country Vocal Performance"). The single was certified platinum, while the album was certified quadruple-platinum and later inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2008.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoe2012147-74)

Meanwhile, two collaborations with Waylon Jennings were released: *[WWII](/wiki/WWII_(album) "WWII (album)")* in 1982,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2005-75) and *[Take it to the Limit](/wiki/Take_It_to_the_Limit_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Take It to the Limit (Willie Nelson album)")* in 1983. In the mid-1980s, Nelson, Jennings, Kristofferson, and [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") formed [The Highwaymen](/wiki/The_Highwaymen_(country_supergroup) "The Highwaymen (country supergroup)"), a supergroup who achieved platinum record sales and toured the world.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008383-76) Meanwhile, Nelson became more involved with charity work, such as singing on "[We Are the World](/wiki/We_Are_the_World "We Are the World")" in 1984.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards2015-77) In 1985, Nelson had another success with *[Half Nelson](/wiki/Half_Nelson_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Half Nelson (Willie Nelson album)")*, a compilation album of duets with a range of artists such as [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") and [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008368-78) In 1980, Nelson performed on the south lawn of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"). The concert of September 13 featured First Lady [Rosalynn Carter](/wiki/Rosalynn_Carter "Rosalynn Carter") and Nelson in a duet of [Ray Wylie Hubbard](/wiki/Ray_Wylie_Hubbard "Ray Wylie Hubbard")'s "Up Against the Wall Redneck Mother". Nelson frequently visited the White House, where, according to the biography by Joe Nick Patoski, *Willie Nelson: An Epic Life*, he smoked marijuana on the White House roof.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008342-79)

### Later career (1990s–2000s)

In 1996, Nelson re-recorded the tracks "[Hello Walls](/wiki/Hello_Walls "Hello Walls")" with the band [the Reverend Horton Heat](/wiki/The_Reverend_Horton_Heat "The Reverend Horton Heat"), and "[Bloody Mary Morning](/wiki/Bloody_Mary_Morning "Bloody Mary Morning")" with the [Supersuckers](/wiki/Supersuckers "Supersuckers"), for
*Twisted Willie*, a tribute album featuring rock versions of Nelson's songs performed by artists such as [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson"), [Jerry Cantrell](/wiki/Jerry_Cantrell "Jerry Cantrell"), [Mark Lanegan](/wiki/Mark_Lanegan "Mark Lanegan"), [L7](/wiki/L7_(band) "L7 (band)"), [the Presidents of the United States of America](/wiki/The_Presidents_of_the_United_States_of_America_(band) "The Presidents of the United States of America (band)"), and [Jello Biafra](/wiki/Jello_Biafra "Jello Biafra"), among others.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHochman1996-80) Proceeds from the sale of the record went to Nelson's Farm Aid.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/President_Bill_Clinton_greets_Willie_Nelson.jpg/250px-President_Bill_Clinton_greets_Willie_Nelson.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Bill_Clinton_greets_Willie_Nelson.jpg)

Nelson with President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") in 1993

During the 1990s and 2000s, Nelson toured continuously, recording several albums including 1998's critically acclaimed *[Teatro](/wiki/Teatro_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Teatro (Willie Nelson album)")*,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008430-82) and performed and recorded with other acts including [Phish](/wiki/Phish "Phish"),[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarm_Aid_Staff2011-83) Johnny Cash,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2000-84) and [Toby Keith](/wiki/Toby_Keith "Toby Keith"). His duet with Keith, "[Beer for My Horses](/wiki/Beer_for_My_Horses "Beer for My Horses")", was released as a single and topped the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_magazine "Billboard magazine")* Hot Country Songs charts for six consecutive weeks in 2003,[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBMI_staff2003-85) while the accompanying video won an award for "Best Video" at the 2004 [Academy of Country Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Country_Music "Academy of Country Music") Awards.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiography_staff2011-86) A [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") television special celebrated Nelson's 70th birthday,[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPareles2003-87) and Nelson released *[The Essential Willie Nelson](/wiki/The_Essential_Willie_Nelson "The Essential Willie Nelson")* as part of the celebration. Nelson also appeared on [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr")'s 2003 album, *[Ringo Rama](/wiki/Ringo_Rama "Ringo Rama")*, as a guest vocal on "Write One for Me".[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEntertainment_One_staff2003-88)

Nelson was featured on the album [*True Love*](/wiki/True_Love_(Toots_%26_the_Maytals_album) "True Love (Toots & the Maytals album)") by [Toots and the Maytals](/wiki/Toots_and_the_Maytals "Toots and the Maytals"), which won the [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") in 2004 for Best Reggae Album, and showcased many notable musicians including [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [Jeff Beck](/wiki/Jeff_Beck "Jeff Beck"), [Trey Anastasio](/wiki/Trey_Anastasio "Trey Anastasio"), [Gwen Stefani](/wiki/Gwen_Stefani "Gwen Stefani"), and [Keith Richards](/wiki/Keith_Richards "Keith Richards").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToots_and_the_Maytals2016-89) In the following year of 2005, Nelson released a [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") album entitled *[Countryman](/wiki/Countryman_(album) "Countryman (album)")* which featured [Toots Hibbert](/wiki/Toots_Hibbert "Toots Hibbert") of [Toots and the Maytals](/wiki/Toots_and_the_Maytals "Toots and the Maytals") on the song "I'm a Worried Man".[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeusner2005-90)

Nelson headlined the 2005 Tsunami Relief Austin to Asia concert to benefit the victims of the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake"), which raised an estimated $75,000 for UNICEF.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_News_staff2005-91) Also in 2005, a live performance of the Johnny Cash song "[Busted](/wiki/Busted_(Johnny_Cash_song) "Busted (Johnny Cash song)")" with Ray Charles was released on Charles' duets album *[Genius & Friends](/wiki/Genius_%26_Friends "Genius & Friends")*. Nelson's 2007 performance with jazz trumpeter [Wynton Marsalis](/wiki/Wynton_Marsalis "Wynton Marsalis") at the [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center "Lincoln Center"), was released as the live album *[Two Men with the Blues](/wiki/Two_Men_with_the_Blues "Two Men with the Blues")* in 2008; reaching number one in *Billboard*'s Top Jazz Albums and number twenty on the *Billboard* 200.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015360-92) The same year, Nelson recorded his first album with [Buddy Cannon](/wiki/Buddy_Cannon "Buddy Cannon") as the producer, *[Moment of Forever](/wiki/Moment_of_Forever "Moment of Forever")*. Cannon acquainted Nelson earlier, during the production of his collaboration with [Kenny Chesney](/wiki/Kenny_Chesney "Kenny Chesney") on the duet "[That Lucky Old Sun](/wiki/That_Lucky_Old_Sun "That Lucky Old Sun")", for Chesney's album *[Lucky Old Sun](/wiki/Lucky_Old_Sun "Lucky Old Sun")*.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooper2014-93) In 2009 Nelson and Marsalis joined with Norah Jones in a tribute concert to Ray Charles, which resulted in the *[Here We Go Again: Celebrating the Genius of Ray Charles](/wiki/Here_We_Go_Again:_Celebrating_the_Genius_of_Ray_Charles "Here We Go Again: Celebrating the Genius of Ray Charles")* album, released in 2011.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie_Nelson.com_staff2011-94)

### 2010s

In 2010, Nelson released *[Country Music](/wiki/Country_Music_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Country Music (Willie Nelson album)")*, a compilation of standards produced by [T-Bone Burnett](/wiki/T-Bone_Burnett "T-Bone Burnett").[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans-Price2010-95) The album peaked at number four in *Billboard*'s Top Country Albums, and twenty on the *Billboard* 200.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2010-96) It was nominated for Best Americana Album at the [2011 Grammy Awards](/wiki/53rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "53rd Annual Grammy Awards").[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECBS_Music_staff2011-97) In 2011 Nelson participated in the concert *Kokua For Japan*, a fund raising event for the victims of the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami") in Japan which raised $1.6 million.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESugimoto2011-98)

In February 2012, [Legacy Recordings](/wiki/Legacy_Recordings "Legacy Recordings") signed a deal with Nelson that included the release of new material, as well as past releases that would be selected and complemented with outtakes and other material selected by him.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie_Nelson.com_staff2012-99) With the new deal, Buddy Cannon returned to produce the recordings of Nelson. After selecting the material and the sound of the tunes with the singer, Cannon's work method consisted in the recording of the tracks with studio musicians, with the takes later completed on a separate session by Nelson with his guitar. Cannon's association to Nelson also extended to songwriting, with singer and producer composing the lyrics by exchanging text messages.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooper2014-93)

Nelson's first release for the Legacy Recordings was *[Heroes](/wiki/Heroes_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Heroes (Willie Nelson album)")*, that included guest appearances by his sons [Lukas](/wiki/Lukas_Nelson_%26_Promise_of_the_Real "Lukas Nelson & Promise of the Real") and Micah of the band [Insects vs Robots](/wiki/Insects_vs_Robots "Insects vs Robots"), Ray Price, Merle Haggard, [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg"), Kris Kristofferson, [Jamey Johnson](/wiki/Jamey_Johnson "Jamey Johnson"), [Billy Joe Shaver](/wiki/Billy_Joe_Shaver "Billy Joe Shaver") and [Sheryl Crow](/wiki/Sheryl_Crow "Sheryl Crow"). The album reached number four on *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*'s [Top Country Albums](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillboard_staff2012-100) His 2013 release *[To All the Girls...](/wiki/To_All_the_Girls... "To All the Girls...")*, a collection of duets with all female partners, featured among others [Dolly Parton](/wiki/Dolly_Parton "Dolly Parton"), [Loretta Lynn](/wiki/Loretta_Lynn "Loretta Lynn"), [Rosanne Cash](/wiki/Rosanne_Cash "Rosanne Cash"), Sheryl Crow, [Mavis Staples](/wiki/Mavis_Staples "Mavis Staples"), Norah Jones, [Emmylou Harris](/wiki/Emmylou_Harris "Emmylou Harris"), [Carrie Underwood](/wiki/Carrie_Underwood "Carrie Underwood") and [Miranda Lambert](/wiki/Miranda_Lambert "Miranda Lambert").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVinson2013-101) The album entered *Billboard*'s Top Country Albums at number two, marking his highest position on the chart since the release of his 1989 *A Horse Called Music*, and extending his record to a total of forty-six top ten albums on the country charts. Nelson scored as well his second top ten album on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), with the release entering at number nine.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJessen2013-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Willie_Nelson%2C_Fort_Wayne%2C_Indiana_%28June_27%2C_2018%29_-_Pamela_Smith_06.jpg/250px-Willie_Nelson%2C_Fort_Wayne%2C_Indiana_%28June_27%2C_2018%29_-_Pamela_Smith_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Nelson,_Fort_Wayne,_Indiana_(June_27,_2018)_-_Pamela_Smith_06.jpg)

Nelson performing in Fort Wayne, Indiana, in 2018

Nelson's following release was *[Band of Brothers](/wiki/Band_of_Brothers_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Band of Brothers (Willie Nelson album)")*, in 2014, the first Nelson album to feature the most newly self-penned songs since 1996's *[Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(Willie_Nelson_album) "Spirit (Willie Nelson album)")*. Upon its release, it topped *Billboard*'s Top Country albums chart, the first time since 1986's *[The Promiseland](/wiki/The_Promiseland "The Promiseland")*, the last Nelson album to top it. The release reached number five on the *Billboard* 200, Nelson's highest position on the chart since 1982's *Always on My Mind*.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeahey2014-103) In December 2014, a duet with [Rhonda Vincent](/wiki/Rhonda_Vincent "Rhonda Vincent"), "Only Me", topped *[Bluegrass Unlimited](/wiki/Bluegrass_Unlimited "Bluegrass Unlimited")'s* National Airplay chart.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolden2014-104) In June 2015, his collaboration with Haggard *[Django and Jimmie](/wiki/Django_and_Jimmie "Django and Jimmie")* topped *Billboard*'s Top Country albums chart and reached number seven on the *Billboard* 200.

In 2017, Nelson released *[God's Problem Child](/wiki/God%27s_Problem_Child "God's Problem Child")*. The release, consisting mostly of Nelson originals co-written with Cannon, entered the Top country albums at number one, while it reached number ten on the *Billboard* 200.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsker2017-105)

In 2018, Nelson sang a song written by [Daniel Lanois](/wiki/Daniel_Lanois "Daniel Lanois") called "Cruel World" for the [soundtrack](/wiki/Music_of_Red_Dead_Redemption_2 "Music of Red Dead Redemption 2") of [Rockstar Games](/wiki/Rockstar_Games "Rockstar Games")'s action-adventure video game *[Red Dead Redemption 2](/wiki/Red_Dead_Redemption_2 "Red Dead Redemption 2")*.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaly2018-106) Lanois wrote the song especially for Nelson. When a hurricane prevented Nelson from recording the song, the production team sent the track to [Josh Homme](/wiki/Josh_Homme "Josh Homme") in the hopes that he could record it in time for the game's release. Nelson was ultimately able to record the song in time in Los Angeles;[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShanley2019-107) the team considered combining the two versions into a duet, but ultimately included both versions in the game.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELanois2019-108) Also in 2018, Nelson was one of several artists on *[Restoration](/wiki/Revamp_%26_Restoration "Revamp & Restoration")*, a cover album containing various country renditions of songs originally by [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), on which he performed "[Border Song](/wiki/Border_Song "Border Song")".[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrickey2018-109) His 2019 release *[Ride Me Back Home](/wiki/Ride_Me_Back_Home "Ride Me Back Home")* charted at number two on the *Billboard* Top Country Albums chart.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsker2019-110) For the title-track, Nelson received the [Grammy Award for Best Country Solo Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Country_Solo_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Country Solo Performance").[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroll2020-111)

### 2020s

Following the [U.S. coronavirus pandemic lockdowns](/wiki/2020_coronavirus_pandemic_in_the_United_States#Lockdowns "2020 coronavirus pandemic in the United States") that began in March 2020, Nelson [livestreamed](/wiki/Livestreaming "Livestreaming") a series of [benefit concerts](/wiki/Benefit_concert "Benefit concert"). The first two raised $700,000 for people who had suffered financial loss due to effects on the U.S. economy.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMargolis2020-112) The third, which was held on April 20, 2020, was a variety show titled *[Come and Toke It](/wiki/Come_and_Toke_It "Come and Toke It")*.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrance2020-113) Some of the content was [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)")-themed, and some of the proceeds will be used to support The Last Prisoner Project, a [restorative justice](/wiki/Restorative_justice "Restorative justice") program relating to persons convicted of cannabis-related crimes.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenthal2020-114)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillman2020-115)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKreps2020-116) The same year, Nelson was approached by [Karen O](/wiki/Karen_O "Karen O") of [The Yeah Yeah Yeahs](/wiki/The_Yeah_Yeah_Yeahs "The Yeah Yeah Yeahs") to collaborate. They chose to perform [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") and [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)")'s "[Under Pressure](/wiki/Under_Pressure "Under Pressure")."[[117]](#cite_note-117)

For his releases of *[The Willie Nelson Family](/wiki/The_Willie_Nelson_Family "The Willie Nelson Family")* (2021) and *[A Beautiful Time](/wiki/A_Beautiful_Time "A Beautiful Time")* (2022), Nelson received four nominations for the [65th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/65th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "65th Annual Grammy Awards"), winning in two categories: Best Country Solo Performance and Best Country Album.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECummings,_Tommy2022-118)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarias,_Robbie2023-119) In December 2022, Mark Rothbaum announced a five-part documentary entitled *Willie Nelson and Family*; it was released at the 2023 [Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECummings,_Tommy2022-118) In 2023, Nelson performed at two concerts at the [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl") celebrating his 90th birthday[[120]](#cite_note-120) and was inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERockHall2023-121)

In 2024, Nelson was featured in the "Smoke Hour ★ Willie Nelson" and "Smoke Hour II" interludes of [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé")'s *[Cowboy Carter](/wiki/Cowboy_Carter "Cowboy Carter")* album, the second album in her [trilogy project](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_discography "Beyoncé discography").[[122]](#cite_note-122) With the death of [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson") in September 2024, Nelson became the last surviving member of  [The Highwaymen](/wiki/The_Highwaymen_(country_supergroup) "The Highwaymen (country supergroup)").

## Other ventures

Nelson's acting debut was in the 1979 film *The Electric Horseman*, followed by appearances in *[Honeysuckle Rose](/wiki/Honeysuckle_Rose_(film) "Honeysuckle Rose (film)")*, *[Thief](/wiki/Thief_(film) "Thief (film)")*, and *[Barbarosa](/wiki/Barbarosa "Barbarosa")*. He played the role of Red Loon in *[Coming Out of the Ice](/wiki/Coming_Out_of_the_Ice "Coming Out of the Ice")* in 1982 and starred in *Songwriter* two years later. He portrayed the lead role in the 1986 film version of his album *Red Headed Stranger*.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllmovie_staff2011-123) Other movies that Nelson acted in include *[Wag the Dog](/wiki/Wag_the_Dog "Wag the Dog")*, *[Gone Fishin'](/wiki/Gone_Fishin%27_(film) "Gone Fishin' (film)")* (as Billy 'Catch' Pooler), the 1986 television movie *[Stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach_(TV_film) "Stagecoach (TV film)")* (with Johnny Cash, Waylon Jennings, and Kris Kristofferson), *[Half Baked](/wiki/Half_Baked "Half Baked")*, *[Beerfest](/wiki/Beerfest "Beerfest")*, *[The Dukes of Hazzard](/wiki/The_Dukes_of_Hazzard_(film) "The Dukes of Hazzard (film)")*, *[Surfer, Dude](/wiki/Surfer,_Dude "Surfer, Dude")* and *[Swing Vote](/wiki/Swing_Vote_(2008_film) "Swing Vote (2008 film)")*. He has also made guest appearances on *[Miami Vice](/wiki/Miami_Vice "Miami Vice")* (1986's "El Viejo" episode); *[Delta](/wiki/Delta_(American_TV_series) "Delta (American TV series)")*; *[Nash Bridges](/wiki/Nash_Bridges "Nash Bridges")*; *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*; *[Monk](/wiki/Monk_(TV_series) "Monk (TV series)")*; *[Adventures in Wonderland](/wiki/Adventures_in_Wonderland_(1992_TV_series) "Adventures in Wonderland (1992 TV series)")*; *[Dr. Quinn, Medicine Woman](/wiki/Dr._Quinn,_Medicine_Woman "Dr. Quinn, Medicine Woman")*; *[King of the Hill](/wiki/King_of_the_Hill_(TV_series) "King of the Hill (TV series)")*; *[The Colbert Report](/wiki/The_Colbert_Report "The Colbert Report")*; *[Swing Vote](/wiki/Swing_Vote "Swing Vote")*; and *[Space Ghost Coast to Coast](/wiki/Space_Ghost_Coast_to_Coast "Space Ghost Coast to Coast")*.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYahoo!_Movies_staff2011-124)

[![A store with a sign that reads "Willie's Place". The apostrophe is replaced in the sign by a bullet hole. The structure of the store is constructed in wooden with three columns. There are four windows and there are a red and a grey car in the parking lot.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Willie%27s_Place_near_Hillsboro%2C_TX_IMG_4050.JPG/250px-Willie%27s_Place_near_Hillsboro%2C_TX_IMG_4050.JPG)](/wiki/File:Willie%27s_Place_near_Hillsboro,_TX_IMG_4050.JPG)

In 2008, Nelson reopened the truck stop [Willie's Place](/wiki/Willie%27s_Place "Willie's Place") near [Hillsboro, Texas](/wiki/Hillsboro,_Texas "Hillsboro, Texas")

In 1988 his first book, *[Willie: An Autobiography](/wiki/Willie:_An_Autobiography "Willie: An Autobiography")*, was published.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake2000-125) *[The Facts of Life: And Other Dirty Jokes](/wiki/The_Facts_of_Life:_And_Other_Dirty_Jokes "The Facts of Life: And Other Dirty Jokes")*, a personal recollection of tour and musical stories from his career, combined with song lyrics, followed in 2002. In 2005 he co-authored *Farm Aid: A Song for America*, a commemorative book about the twentieth anniversary of the foundation of Farm Aid.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorge-WarrenHoekstra2005-126) His third book, co-authored with long-time friend [Turk Pipkin](/wiki/Turk_Pipkin "Turk Pipkin"), *The Tao of Willie: A Guide to the Happiness in Your Heart*, was published in 2006.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENPR_staff2010-127) In 2007 a book advocating the use of bio-diesel and the reduction of gas emissions, *On the Clean Road Again: Biodiesel and the Future of the Family Farm*, was published.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2007a29-4) His next book, *A Tale Out of Luck*, published in 2008 and co-authored by Mike Blakely, was Nelson's first fictional book.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2008-128) In 2012, a new autobiography by Nelson, *[Roll Me Up and Smoke Me When I Die: Musings from the Road](/wiki/Roll_Me_Up_and_Smoke_Me_When_I_Die:_Musings_from_the_Road "Roll Me Up and Smoke Me When I Die: Musings from the Road")*, was announced. Released on November 13, it was named after the song from his album *Heroes*. The book contained further biographical details, as well as family pictures and stories about Nelson's political views, as well as his marijuana advocacy. The artwork of the book was designed by Nelson's son, Micah, and the foreword written by Kinky Friedman.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitaker2012-129) A second Nelson autobiography, *[It's a Long Story: My Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Long_Story:_My_Life "It's a Long Story: My Life")* co-authored with [David Ritz](/wiki/David_Ritz "David Ritz"), was published on May 5, 2015.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHachette_staff2015-130) *[Pretty Paper](/wiki/Pretty_Paper_(book) "Pretty Paper (book)")*, another collaboration with Ritz, was published the following year.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2016-131)

In 2002, Nelson became the official spokesman of the [Texas Roadhouse](/wiki/Texas_Roadhouse "Texas Roadhouse"), a chain of steakhouses. Nelson heavily promoted the chain and appeared on a special on [Food Network](/wiki/Food_Network "Food Network"). The chain installed *Willie's Corner*, a section dedicated to him and decked out with Willie memorabilia, at several locations.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarey2003-132) In 2004, Nelson and his wife Annie became partners with Bob and Kelly King in the building of two Pacific bio-diesel plants, one in [Salem, Oregon](/wiki/Salem,_Oregon "Salem, Oregon"), and the other at Carl's Corner, Texas (the Texas plant was founded by Carl Cornelius, a longtime Nelson friend and the eponym for Carl's Corner). In 2005, Nelson and several other business partners formed [Willie Nelson Biodiesel](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_Biodiesel "Willie Nelson Biodiesel") ("Bio-Willie"), a company that markets biodiesel bio-fuel to truck stops.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithTaylor2008173-133) The fuel is made from vegetable oil (mainly soybean oil) and can be burned without modification in [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine").[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAssociated_Press_staff2005-134) Nelson registered his company with Earth Biofuels and he became a board member. In 2007, Nelson forfeited six million shares of the company as he left the board.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrick,_Michael2014-135) By 2008, he reopened [Willie's Place](/wiki/Willie%27s_Place "Willie's Place"), a truck stop in [Carl's Corner, Texas](/wiki/Carl%27s_Corner,_Texas "Carl's Corner, Texas"). The [U.S. Bankruptcy Court](/wiki/U.S._Bankruptcy_Court "U.S. Bankruptcy Court") allowed Nelson to invest in it. The establishment had about 80 employees,[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2011-136) and was used as a concert hall with a bar and a 1,000 square feet (93 m2) dance floor.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie's_Place_staff2010-137) Willie's Place also featured BioWillie pumps. At the time, the government of the United States offered a one dollar tax credit to producers of biodiesel. As the competition grew, the tax credit was discontinued while the prices of fossil fuels continued to drop. Nelson and his partners defaulted the $4.75 million loan they took for the construction of Willie's Place. Nelson then paid $35,000 back, while a judge later rejected the request of his creditors to receive more money from him. [TravelCenters of America](/wiki/TravelCenters_of_America "TravelCenters of America") bought Willie's Place during its foreclosure in 2011. The BioWillie plant was then put on the market for sale. Nelson kept the trademark for BioWillie, which is sold on the islands of Maui and Hawaii. Meanwhile, Nelson owns shares of Big Island Biodiesel in Hawaii and SeQuential-Pacific Biodiesel in Oregon.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrick,_Michael2014-135)

In 2010, Nelson founded, with the collaboration of producers and filmmakers [Luck Films](/wiki/Luck_Films "Luck Films"), a company dedicated to producing feature films, documentaries, and concerts.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChagollan2010-138) The next year, he created the [Willie's Roadhouse](/wiki/Willie%27s_Roadhouse "Willie's Roadhouse") show which aired on channel 56 of [SiriusXM](/wiki/SiriusXM "SiriusXM") radio. The channel was a result of the merger of his two other channels *The Roadhouse* and *Willie's Place*.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpry.com_staff2011-139) In November 2014, it was announced that Nelson would be the host of the television series *Inside Arlyn*, shot at Arlyn Studio in Austin, Texas. The thirteen-episode first season would feature artists being interviewed by Nelson and [Dan Rather](/wiki/Dan_Rather "Dan Rather"), followed by a performance. The series concept received attention from cable channels that requested to see the pilot episode.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwiatecki2014-140) Following the legalization of marijuana in different states, Nelson announced in 2015 through spokesman Michael Bowman the establishment of his own marijuana brand, [Willie's Reserve](/wiki/Willie%27s_Reserve "Willie's Reserve"). Plans to open chain stores in the states where marijuana was legalized were announced, to be expanded state-to-state if marijuana legalization further expanded. Bowman called the brand "a culmination of (Nelson's) vision, and his whole life".[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuardian_Music_staff2015-141)

In 2017, Nelson appeared as himself in [Woody Harrelson](/wiki/Woody_Harrelson "Woody Harrelson")'s live film, *[Lost in London](/wiki/Lost_in_London "Lost in London")*.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELodderhose2016-142) In June 2017, he appeared alongside Merle Haggard in the documentary *[The American Epic Sessions](/wiki/The_American_Epic_Sessions "The American Epic Sessions")* directed by [Bernard MacMahon](/wiki/Bernard_MacMahon_(filmmaker) "Bernard MacMahon (filmmaker)"). They performed a song Haggard had composed for the film, "The Only Man Wilder Than Me",[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESolomon2017-143)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHautzinger2017-144) and [Bob Wills](/wiki/Bob_Wills "Bob Wills")'s classic "Old Fashioned Love",[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHautzinger2017-144) which they recorded live direct to disc on the first [electrical sound recording](/wiki/History_of_sound_recording#The_electrical_era_(1925_to_1945)_(including_sound_on_film) "History of sound recording") system from the 1920s.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELocke2017-145) It was the last filmed performance of the pair. *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* commented that "in the final performance of Sessions, Willie Nelson and Merle Haggard perform the duet 'The Only Man Wilder Than Me.' Haggard has a look of complete joy on his face throughout the session in the old-timey recording setup once used by his musical heroes."[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAppleford2017-146)

## Musical style

Nelson uses a variety of music styles to create his own distinctive blend of country music, a hybrid of jazz, pop, blues, rock and folk.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2015-147) His style incorporates a "relaxed, behind-the-beat singing style and gut-string guitar."[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEncyclopædia_Britannica_staff2011-148) His "nasal voice and jazzy, off-center phrasing"[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2015-147) has been responsible for his wide appeal, and has made him a "vital icon in country music", influencing the "[new country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [new traditionalist](/wiki/Neotraditional_country "Neotraditional country"), and [alternative country](/wiki/Alternative_country "Alternative country") movements of the 1980s and 1990s".[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErlewine2015-147)

### Guitars

Main article: [Trigger (guitar)](/wiki/Trigger_(guitar) "Trigger (guitar)")

[![A classical guitar. There are several damages in the soundboard, near the sound hole there is a big hole and the wood is worn out in the surrounding areas of it. The guitar has several signatures on it. there is a blue and white strap in the soundhole.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Trigger-Willie_Nelson.jpg/250px-Trigger-Willie_Nelson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trigger-Willie_Nelson.jpg)

Nelson's guitar, Trigger, has been signed by several of Nelson's friends

In 1969, the Baldwin company gave Nelson an amplifier and guitar with their "Prismatone" [pickup](/wiki/Pickup_(music_technology) "Pickup (music technology)"). During a show in [Helotes, Texas](/wiki/Helotes,_Texas "Helotes, Texas"), Nelson left the guitar on the floor of the stage, and it was later stepped on by a drunk man.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2005-149)
He sent it to be repaired in Nashville by [Shot Jackson](/wiki/Shot_Jackson "Shot Jackson"), who told Nelson that the damage was too great. Jackson offered him a [Martin](/wiki/C._F._Martin_%26_Company "C. F. Martin & Company") N-20 [classical guitar](/wiki/Classical_guitar "Classical guitar"), and, at Nelson's request, moved the pickup to the Martin. Nelson purchased the guitar unseen for $750 and named it after [Roy Rogers](/wiki/Roy_Rogers "Roy Rogers")' horse ["Trigger"](/wiki/Trigger_(horse) "Trigger (horse)").[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie_Nelson_General_Store_staff2010-150) The next year Nelson rescued the guitar from his burning ranch.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReinert1976103-151)[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDraper1991103-152)

Constant strumming with a [guitar pick](/wiki/Guitar_pick "Guitar pick") over the decades has worn a large sweeping hole into the guitar's body near the sound hole—the N-20 has no [pickguard](/wiki/Pickguard "Pickguard") since classical guitars are meant to be played fingerstyle instead of with picks.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENPR_staff1996-37) Its soundboard has been signed by over a hundred of Nelson's friends and associates, ranging from fellow musicians to lawyers and football coaches.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie_Nelson_General_Store_staff2010-150) The first signature on the guitar was [Leon Russell](/wiki/Leon_Russell "Leon Russell")'s, who asked Nelson initially to sign his guitar. When Nelson was about to sign it with a marker, Russell requested him to scratch it instead, explaining that the guitar would be more valuable in the future. Interested in the concept, Nelson requested Russell to also sign his guitar.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2005-149) In 1991, during his process with the IRS, Nelson was worried that Trigger could be auctioned off, stating: "When Trigger goes, I'll quit". He asked his daughter, Lana, to take the guitar from the studio before any IRS agent arrived there, and then deliver it to him in [Maui](/wiki/Maui "Maui").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDraper1991103-152) Nelson then concealed the guitar in his manager's house until his debt was paid off in 1993.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillie_Nelson_General_Store_staff2010-150)

## Political views and activism

Nelson has supported a number of causes. Along with [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young") and [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp"), he set up [Farm Aid](/wiki/Farm_Aid "Farm Aid") in 1985 to assist and increase awareness of the importance of family farms, after [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan")'s comments during the [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concert that he hoped some of the money would help American farmers in danger of losing their farms through mortgage debt.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDurchholzGraff2010134-153) The first concert included Dylan, [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel"), [B.B. King](/wiki/B.B._King "B.B. King"), [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison"), and Neil Young among many others, and raised over $9 million for America's family farmers.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDurchholzGraff2010135-154) Besides organizing and performing in the annual concerts, Nelson is the president of the board of Farm Aid.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichmond200094-155)

Nelson is a co-chair of the [National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws](/wiki/National_Organization_for_the_Reform_of_Marijuana_Laws "National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws") (NORML) advisory board.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENORML_staff2009-156) He has worked with NORML for years, promoting marijuana legalization. In 2005 Nelson and his family hosted the first annual "Willie Nelson & NORML Benefit Golf Tournament", leading to a cover appearance and inside interview in the January 2008 issue of *[High Times](/wiki/High_Times "High Times")* magazine.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHigh_Times_staff2007-157) After Nelson's arrest for possession of marijuana in 2010, he created Willie Nelson's TeaPot Party under the motto "Tax it, regulate it and legalize it!"[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomson2010-158) Nelson got 19 write-in votes for President of the United States in the Minnesota [Legal Marijuana Now](/wiki/Legal_Marijuana_Now_Party "Legal Marijuana Now Party") presidential [primary held on March 5](/wiki/2024_Minnesota_Legal_Marijuana_Now_presidential_primary "2024 Minnesota Legal Marijuana Now presidential primary"), 2024.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

In 2001, following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), Nelson participated in the benefit telethon *[America: A Tribute to Heroes](/wiki/America:_A_Tribute_to_Heroes "America: A Tribute to Heroes")*, leading the rest of the celebrities singing the song "[America the Beautiful](/wiki/America_the_Beautiful "America the Beautiful")".[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuayDamico2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidlx7i4YHl_NoCpgPA149_149]-160) In 2010, during an interview with [Larry King](/wiki/Larry_King "Larry King"), Nelson expressed his [doubts](/wiki/9/11_conspiracy_theories "9/11 conspiracy theories") with regards to the attacks and the official story. Nelson explained that he could not believe that the buildings could collapse due to the planes, attributing instead the result to an implosion.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Reilly2010-161)

[![A man with long white hair and white beard playing a guitar. He wears a black T-shirt, which is crossed by the red, white and blue strap of the guitar. He also wears black pants.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Willie_Nelson_930_club_2012_-_12.jpg/250px-Willie_Nelson_930_club_2012_-_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Nelson_930_club_2012_-_12.jpg)

Nelson, and his guitar "[Trigger](/wiki/Trigger_(guitar) "Trigger (guitar)")", performing in 2012

Nelson supported [Dennis Kucinich](/wiki/Dennis_Kucinich "Dennis Kucinich")'s campaign in the [2004 Democratic presidential primaries](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States)_presidential_primaries,_2004 "Democratic Party (United States) presidential primaries, 2004"). He raised money, appeared at events, and composed the song "Whatever Happened to Peace on Earth?", criticizing the [war in Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReuters_staff2004-162) He recorded a radio advertisement asking for support to put musician/author [Kinky Friedman](/wiki/Kinky_Friedman "Kinky Friedman") on the ballot as an independent candidate for the [2006 Texas gubernatorial election](/wiki/2006_Texas_gubernatorial_election "2006 Texas gubernatorial election").[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamilton2010-163) Friedman promised Nelson a job in Austin as the head of a new Texas Energy Commission due to his support of bio-fuels.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurt2005-164) In January 2008, Nelson filed a suit against the [Texas Democratic Party](/wiki/Texas_Democratic_Party "Texas Democratic Party"), alleging that the party violated the [First](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") and [Fourteenth Amendments](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") of the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") by refusing to allow co-plaintiff Kucinich to appear on the primary ballot because he had scratched out part of the [loyalty oath](/wiki/Loyalty_oath "Loyalty oath") on his application.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelby2009-165)

Nelson is an advocate for better treatment for horses and has been campaigning for the passage of the [American Horse Slaughter Prevention Act](/wiki/Horse_slaughter#Federal_bills "Horse slaughter") (H.R. 503/S. 311) alongside the [Animal Welfare Institute](/wiki/Animal_Welfare_Institute "Animal Welfare Institute").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2007b-166) He is on its board of directors and has adopted a number of horses from *Habitat for Horses*.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabitat_for_Horses_staff2011-167) In 2008, Nelson signed on to warn consumers about the cruel and illegal living conditions for calves raised to produce milk for dairy products. He wrote letters to [Land O'Lakes](/wiki/Land_O%27Lakes "Land O'Lakes") and Challenge Dairy, two of the major corporations that use milk from calves raised at California's Mendes Calf Ranch, which employs an intensive confinement practice that was the subject of a lawsuit and campaign brought by the [Animal Legal Defense Fund](/wiki/Animal_Legal_Defense_Fund "Animal Legal Defense Fund").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson2008-128) Nelson is seen in the film *[The Garden](/wiki/The_Garden_(2008_film) "The Garden (2008 film)")* supporting the impoverished community South Central Farm in Southern Los Angeles.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2019-168)

A supporter of the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") movement, Nelson published in 2006 through [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") a version of [Ned Sublette](/wiki/Ned_Sublette "Ned Sublette")'s "[Cowboys Are Frequently, Secretly Fond of Each Other](/wiki/Cowboys_Are_Frequently,_Secretly_Fond_of_Each_Other "Cowboys Are Frequently, Secretly Fond of Each Other")", that met instant success.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeu2006-169) During an interview with *[Texas Monthly](/wiki/Texas_Monthly "Texas Monthly")* in 2013, regarding the [Defense of Marriage Act](/wiki/Defense_of_Marriage_Act "Defense of Marriage Act") and [same-sex marriage in the United States](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States"), Nelson responded to a comparison the interviewer made with the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement "Civil Rights Movement"), stating: "We'll look back and say it was crazy that we ever even argued about this". He also presented two logos with the pink equal sign, symbol of the LGBT movement. The first one, featured the sign represented with two long braids; while the second one, featured the sign represented with two marijuana cigarettes. The use of the logos became popular quickly in social networking sites such as Twitter and Facebook.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELanger2013-170)
