In June 2018, Nelson deplored the [Trump administration family separation policy](/wiki/Trump_administration_family_separation_policy "Trump administration family separation policy").[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHudak2018-171) During his Fourth of July Picnic, he performed a song with [Beto O'Rourke](/wiki/Beto_O%27Rourke "Beto O'Rourke"), the Democratic candidate for the [Senate election in Texas](/wiki/United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas,_2018 "United States Senate election in Texas, 2018"). Nelson endorsed O'Rourke, and received negative reactions from the conservative part of his followers.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2018-172) On September 29, 2018, Nelson offered a free concert in Austin supporting the candidate's run. The last number he performed was "[Vote 'Em Out](/wiki/Vote_%27Em_Out "Vote 'Em Out")", a new track that was subsequently released as a single.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHudak2018-171)

## Personal life

Nelson has been married four times and has eight children.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHollabaugh2010-173) His first marriage was to Martha Matthews from 1952 to 1962. The couple had three children: Lana, Susie, and Willie "Billy" Hugh Jr. The latter died by suicide in 1991.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall2008-174) The marriage was marked by violence, with Matthews assaulting Nelson several times,[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECartwright2000[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidFBRbZWBZolUCpgPA276_276]-175) including one incident when she sewed him up in bedsheets and beat him with a broomstick.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldman2012-176) Nelson then married [Shirley Collie](/wiki/Shirley_Collie "Shirley Collie") in 1963. The couple divorced in 1971, after Collie found a bill from the maternity ward of a Houston hospital charged to Nelson and Connie Koepke for the birth of [Paula Carlene Nelson](/wiki/Paula_Nelson "Paula Nelson").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECartwright2000[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidFBRbZWBZolUCpgPA276_276]-175) Nelson married Koepke the same year, and they had another daughter, Amy Lee Nelson. Following a divorce in 1988, he married his current wife, Annie D'Angelo, in 1991. They have two sons, [Lukas Autry](/wiki/Lukas_Nelson_%26_Promise_of_the_Real "Lukas Nelson & Promise of the Real") and Jacob Micah.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiggs2007239-177) In 2012, Nelson learned that he fathered a daughter with his friend Mary Haney. Born on January 22, 1953, Rene Butts (born Lynda Renee Barley) met Nelson and she shared a picture of both of them on Father's Day in 2016. Butts died in 2017.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrauser,_Emily2023-178)

Nelson owns "Luck, Texas", a ranch in [Spicewood](/wiki/Spicewood,_Texas "Spicewood, Texas"),[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERolling_Stone_staff2017-179)[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2014-180) and also lives in Maui, Hawaii[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKane2008-181) with several celebrity neighbors.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrigoriadis200757-182) While swimming in Hawaii in 1981, Nelson's [lung collapsed](/wiki/Pneumothorax "Pneumothorax"). He was taken to the Maui Memorial Hospital and his scheduled concerts were canceled.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrebs1981-183) Nelson temporarily stopped smoking cigarettes each time his lungs became congested, and resumed when the congestion ended.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Hare2010-184) He was then smoking between two and three packs per day. After suffering from pneumonia several times, he decided to quit either marijuana or tobacco. He chose to quit tobacco.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENPR_staff2012-185) In 2008, he started to smoke marijuana with a carbon-free system to avoid the effects of smoke.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2011-186) In 2004, Nelson underwent surgery for [carpal tunnel syndrome](/wiki/Carpal_tunnel_syndrome "Carpal tunnel syndrome"), as he had damaged his wrists by continuously playing the guitar.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAssociated_Press_staff2002-187) On the recommendation of his doctor, he canceled his scheduled concerts and only wrote songs during his recovery.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller_Loncaric2008-188) In 2012, he canceled a fund-raising appearance in the Denver area. He suffered from breathing problems due to high altitude and emphysema and was taken to a local hospital. His publicist Elaine Schock confirmed soon after that Nelson's health was good and that he was heading to his next scheduled concert in Dallas, Texas.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKinnley2012-189) After repeated instances of pneumonia and emphysema through the years, Nelson underwent [stem-cell therapy](/wiki/Stem-cell_therapy "Stem-cell therapy") in 2015 to improve the state of his lungs.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeim2015-190)

During his childhood, Nelson grew interested in martial arts. He ordered self-defense manuals on [jujitsu](/wiki/Jujitsu "Jujitsu") and judo that he saw advertised in *[Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman")* and *[Superman](/wiki/Superman "Superman")* comic books. Nelson started to formally practice [kung fu](/wiki/Kung_fu "Kung fu") after he moved to Nashville, in the 1960s.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonShrakeShrake200055-191) During the 1980s, Nelson began training in [taekwondo](/wiki/Tae_kwon_do "Tae kwon do") and now holds a second-degree black belt in that discipline.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilton2012-192) During the 1990s, Nelson started to practice the Korean martial art [GongKwon Yusul](/wiki/GongKwon_Yusul "GongKwon Yusul").[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall2014-193) In 2014, after 20 years in the discipline, his Grand Master Sam Um presented him with a fifth-degree black belt in a ceremony held in Austin, Texas.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilton2014-194) A 2014 *[Tae Kwon Do Times](/wiki/Tae_Kwon_Do_Times "Tae Kwon Do Times")* magazine interview revealed that Nelson had developed an unorthodox manner of training during the lengthy periods of time he was on tour. Nelson would conduct his martial arts training on his tour bus "The Honeysuckle Rose" and send videos to his supervising Master for review and critique.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZirogiannis2014-195)

In March 2021, Nelson recorded a version of the popular standard "[I'll Be Seeing You](/wiki/I%27ll_Be_Seeing_You_(song) "I'll Be Seeing You (song)")" that was used on a [public service announcement](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement") by the [Ad Council](/wiki/Ad_Council "Ad Council") to encourage [COVID-19 vaccination in the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 vaccination in the United States").[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisholtz,_Drew2021-196) Earlier in January 2021, Nelson and his sister received the [Moderna COVID-19 vaccine](/wiki/Moderna_COVID-19_vaccine "Moderna COVID-19 vaccine").[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle,_Patrick2021-197) In May 2022, Nelson postponed several shows after he contracted [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"). Nelson's wife detailed that he was severely affected by the illness, and that he received a treatment including [Paxlovid](/wiki/Paxlovid "Paxlovid"), [monoclonal antibodies](/wiki/Monoclonal_antibody "Monoclonal antibody"), and steroids. Nelson resumed his tour in September 2022.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein,_Tiffany2022-198) During June 2024, Nelson had to cancel his performances at Outlaw Music Festival due to health complications and was ordered to rest for four days by a doctor.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

### Legal issues

Nelson has been arrested several times for marijuana possession. The first occasion was in 1974 in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), Texas.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoddard2010-200) In 1977, after a tour with Hank Cochran, Nelson traveled to [The Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"). Nelson and Cochran arrived late to the airport and boarded the flight without luggage.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015247-201) The bags were later sent to them. As Nelson and Cochran claimed their luggage in the Bahamas, a customs officer questioned Nelson after marijuana was found in a pair of his jeans. Nelson was arrested and jailed. As Cochran made arrangements to pay the bail, he took Nelson a six-pack of beer to his cell.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015248-202) Nelson was released a few hours later. Inebriated, he fell after he jumped celebrating and was taken to the emergency room. He then appeared before the judge, who dropped the charges but ordered Nelson to never return to the country.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelsonRitz2015249-203)

In 1994, [Texas Highway patrolmen](/wiki/Texas_Highway_Patrol "Texas Highway Patrol") found marijuana in his car near Waco, Texas. His requirement to appear in court prevented him from attending the Grammy awards that year.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2011-186) While traveling to [Ann W. Richards](/wiki/Ann_W._Richards "Ann W. Richards")' funeral in 2006, Nelson, along with his manager and his sister, Bobbie, were arrested in [St. Martin Parish, Louisiana](/wiki/St._Martin_Parish,_Louisiana "St. Martin Parish, Louisiana"), and charged with possession of marijuana and hallucinogenic mushrooms.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeople_staff2006-204) Nelson received six months probation.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008471-205)

On November 26, 2010, Nelson was arrested in [Sierra Blanca, Texas](/wiki/Sierra_Blanca,_Texas "Sierra Blanca, Texas"), for possession of six ounces of marijuana found in his tour bus while traveling from Los Angeles back to Texas. He was released after paying bail of $2,500.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECline2010-206) Prosecutor Kit Bramblett supported not sentencing Nelson to jail due to the small amount of marijuana involved, but suggested instead a $100 fine and told Nelson that he would have him sing "Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain" for the court. Judge Becky Dean-Walker said that Nelson would have to pay the fine but not to perform the song, explaining that the prosecutor was joking.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaulfield2011-207) Nelson's lawyer Joe Turner reached an agreement with the prosecutor. Nelson was set to pay a $500 fine to avoid a two-year jail sentence with a 30-day review period, which in case of another incident would end the agreement.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmter2011-208) The judge later rejected the agreement, claiming that Nelson was receiving preferential treatment for his celebrity status; the offense normally carried a one-year jail sentence.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETMZ_staff2011-209) Bramblett declared that the case would remain open until it was either dismissed or the judge changed her opinion.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen2012-210)

### Issues with the Internal Revenue Service

In 1990, the IRS seized most of Nelson's assets, claiming that he owed $32 million. In addition to the unpaid taxes, Nelson's situation was worsened by the weak investments he had made during the early 1980s.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDraper1991177-211) In 1978, after Nelson fired his manager Neil Reshen, Dallas lawyer Terry Bray introduced Nelson to the accounting firm [Price Waterhouse](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers"). To repay the debt Reshen had created with the IRS, Nelson was recommended to invest in tax shelters that ultimately flopped.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008407-212) While the IRS disallowed his deductions for 1980, 1981 and 1982 (at a time that Nelson's income multiplied),[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008407-212) due to penalties and interests, the debt increased by the end of the decade.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatoski2008406-213)

His lawyer, [Jay Goldberg](/wiki/Jay_Goldberg "Jay Goldberg"), negotiated the sum to be lowered to $16 million. Later, Nelson's attorney renegotiated a settlement with the IRS in which he paid $6 million, although Nelson did not comply with the agreement.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDraper1991177-211) Nelson released *[The IRS Tapes: Who'll Buy My Memories?](/wiki/The_IRS_Tapes:_Who%27ll_Buy_My_Memories%3F "The IRS Tapes: Who'll Buy My Memories?")* as a double album, with all profits destined for the IRS. Many of his assets were auctioned and purchased by friends, who donated or rented his possessions to him for a nominal fee. He sued Price Waterhouse, contending that they put his money in illegal tax shelters.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECowan1991-214) The lawsuit was settled for an undisclosed amount and Nelson cleared his debts by 1993.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston1995-215)

## Legacy

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Willie Nelson](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Willie_Nelson "List of awards and nominations received by Willie Nelson")

[![A sign of a street that reads "2nd street, Willie Nelson BLVD 100". It is night time and the sign is lighted. The borders and letters are white and the inside is red.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Willie_Nelson_BLVD.jpg/250px-Willie_Nelson_BLVD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Nelson_BLVD.jpg)

Willie Nelson boulevard in Austin, Texas

Nelson is widely recognized as an American icon.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPBS_staff2011-216)[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamilton2010-163) He was inducted into the [Country Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Country_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Country Music Hall of Fame") in 1993,[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1993-217) and he received the [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors") in 1998.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy_Center_staff2011-218) In 2011, Nelson was inducted to the [National Agricultural Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Agricultural_Center_and_Hall_of_Fame "National Agricultural Center and Hall of Fame"), for his labor in Farm Aid and other fund raisers to benefit farmers.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreolo2011-219) In 2015 Nelson won the [Gershwin Prize](/wiki/Gershwin_Prize "Gershwin Prize"), the lifetime award of the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress").[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeahey2015-220) In 2018, [The Texas Institute of Letters](/wiki/Texas_Institute_of_Letters "Texas Institute of Letters") inducted him among its members for his songwriting.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMerschel2018-221) He was included by *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone_(magazine) "Rolling Stone (magazine)")* on its *100 Greatest Singers* and *100 Greatest Guitarists* lists.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERolling_Stone_staff2010-222)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERolling_Stone_staff2011-223)

In 2003, Texas Governor Perry signed bill No. 2582, introduced by State Representative [Elizabeth Ames Jones](/wiki/Elizabeth_Ames_Jones "Elizabeth Ames Jones") and Senator [Jeff Wentworth](/wiki/Jeff_Wentworth "Jeff Wentworth"), which funded the *Texas Music Project*, the state's official music charity. Nelson was named honorary chairman of the advisory board of the project.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETexas_Music_Project_staff2011-224) In 2005, Democratic Texas Senator [Gonzalo Barrientos](/wiki/Gonzalo_Barrientos "Gonzalo Barrientos") introduced a bill to name 49 miles (79 km) of the [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas") section of [State Highway 130](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_130 "Texas State Highway 130") after Nelson, and at one point 23 of the 31 state senators were co-sponsors of the bill.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2005-225) The legislation was dropped after two Republican senators, [Florence Shapiro](/wiki/Florence_Shapiro "Florence Shapiro") and Wentworth, objected, citing Nelson's lack of connection to the highway, his fund raisers for Democrats, his drinking, and his marijuana advocacy.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFort_Worth_Star-telegram_staff2005-226)

An important collection of Willie Nelson materials (1975–1994) became part of the [Wittliff collections](/wiki/Wittliff_collections "Wittliff collections") of Southwestern Writers, [Texas State University](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University"), [San Marcos, Texas](/wiki/San_Marcos,_Texas "San Marcos, Texas"). The collection contains lyrics, screenplays, letters, concert programs, tour itineraries, posters, articles, clippings, personal effects, promotional items, souvenirs, and documents. It documents Nelson's IRS troubles and how Farm Aid contributions were used. Most of the material was collected by Nelson's friend [Bill Wittliff](/wiki/Bill_Wittliff "Bill Wittliff"), who wrote or co-wrote *Honeysuckle Rose*, *Barbarosa* and *Red Headed Stranger*.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkek_Library_staff2010-227) In 2014, Nelson donated his personal collection to the [Dolph Briscoe Center for American History](/wiki/Dolph_Briscoe_Center_for_American_History "Dolph Briscoe Center for American History"). The items include photographs, correspondence, song manuscripts, posters, certificate records, awards, signed books, screenplays, personal items and gifts and tributes from Nelson's fans.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriscoe_Center_staff2014-228)

In April 2010, Nelson received the "Feed the Peace" award from [The Nobelity Project](/wiki/The_Nobelity_Project "The Nobelity Project") for his extensive work with Farm Aid and overall contributions to world peace. On June 23, 2010, he was inducted into the Library of Congress's [National Recording Registry](/wiki/National_Recording_Registry "National Recording Registry").[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonahue2010-229) Nelson is an honorary trustee of the [Dayton International Peace Museum](/wiki/Dayton_International_Peace_Museum "Dayton International Peace Museum").[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDayton_Peace_Museum_staff2010-230)
In 2010, Austin, Texas renamed Second Street to Willie Nelson Boulevard. The city also unveiled [a life-size statue](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_statue "Willie Nelson statue") to honor him, placed at the entrance of *Austin City Limits'* new studio.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_News_staff2010-231) The non-profit organization Capital Area Statues commissioned sculptor Clete Shields to execute the project.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHouston_Chronicle_staff2010-232) The statue was unveiled on April 20, 2012.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomlinson2012a-233) The date selected by the city of Austin unintentionally coincided with the number 4/20, [associated with cannabis culture](/wiki/420_(cannabis_culture) "420 (cannabis culture)"). In spite of the coincidence and Nelson's advocacy for the legalization of marijuana, the ceremony was scheduled also for 4:20 pm. During the ceremony, Nelson performed the song "[Roll Me Up and Smoke Me When I Die](/wiki/Roll_Me_Up_and_Smoke_Me_When_I_Die "Roll Me Up and Smoke Me When I Die")".[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomlinson2012b-234) The same year, Nelson was honored during the 46th Annual Country Music Association Awards as the first recipient of the [Lifetime Achievement Award](/wiki/Willie_Nelson_Lifetime_Achievement_Award "Willie Nelson Lifetime Achievement Award"), which was also named after him.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis2012-235) In 2013, he received an honorary doctorate from the [Berklee College of Music](/wiki/Berklee_College_of_Music "Berklee College of Music").[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2013-236) The following year, he was part of the inaugural class inducted into the *Austin City Limits* Hall of Fame. Also included among the first inductees was his friend Darrell Royal, whose jamming parties that Nelson participated in were the source of inspiration for the show.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAssociated_Press_staff2014-237)

For many years, Nelson's image was marked by his red hair, often divided into two long braids partially concealed under a bandanna. In the April 2007 issue of [Stuff Magazine](/wiki/Stuff_Magazine "Stuff Magazine") Nelson was interviewed about his long locks.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett2010-238) "I started braiding my hair when it started getting too long, and that was, I don't know, probably in the 70's." On May 26, 2010, the Associated Press reported that Nelson had cut his hair,[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAssociated_Press_staff2010-239) and Nashville music journalist Jimmy Carter published a photograph of the pigtail-free Nelson on his website. Nelson wanted a more maintainable hairstyle, as well helping him stay cool more easily at his Maui home.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter2010-240) In October 2014, the braids of Nelson were sold for $37,000 at an auction of the Waylon Jennings estate. In 1983, Nelson cut his braids and gave them to Jennings as a gift during a party celebrating Jennings' sobriety.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENew_York_Post_staff2014-241)

Nelson's touring and recording group, the Family, is full of longstanding members. The original lineup included his sister Bobbie Nelson, drummer Paul English, harmonicist [Mickey Raphael](/wiki/Mickey_Raphael "Mickey Raphael"), bassist Bee Spears, drummer/percussionist Billy English (Paul's younger brother), and guitarist [Jody Payne](/wiki/Jody_Payne "Jody Payne").[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobey1982198,_358-242) The current lineup only includes Nelson himself, Raphael, and Billy English from the classic lineup, as Jody Payne retired (before dying in 2013), Bee Spears died in 2011, Paul English died in 2020, and Willie's sister [Bobbie Nelson](/wiki/Bobbie_Nelson "Bobbie Nelson") died in 2022.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKerns2012-243) *Willie & Family* tours North America in the bio-diesel bus *Honeysuckle Rose*, which is fueled by Bio-Willie.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancouver_Province_staff2007-244) Nelson's tour buses were customized by Florida Coach since 1979. The company built the *Honeysuckle Rose I* in 1983, which was replaced after a collision in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), Canada, in 1990. The interior was salvaged and reused for the second version of the bus the same year. Nelson changed his tour bus in 1996, 2005 and 2013, and is touring on the *Honeysuckle Rose V*.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELanger2014-245)

## Bibliography

* *Willie: An Autobiography*, Simon & Schuster, 1988, with [Bud Shrake](/wiki/Bud_Shrake "Bud Shrake")
* *The Facts of Life and Other Dirty Jokes*, Random House, 2002
* *Farm Aid: A Song for America*, Rodale Books, foreword by Willie Nelson, 2005
* *The Tao of Willie: A Guide to the Happiness in Your Heart*, Gotham, 2006, with Turk Pipkin
* *On the Clean Road Again: Biodiesel and the Future of the Family Farm*, Fulcrum Publishing, 2007
* *A Tale Out of Luck* (a novel), Center Street, 2008, with Mike Blakely
* *Roll Me Up and Smoke Me When I Die: Musings From the Road*, William Morrow, foreword by Kinky Friedman, 2012
* *It's a Long Story: My Life*, Little, Brown and Company, 2015 with David Ritz
* *Pretty Paper*, Penguin Random House, 2016 with David Ritz
* *Willie Nelson's Letters to America*, HarperCollins, 2021 with Turk Pipkin
* *Me and Sister Bobbie: True Tales of the Family Band*, Penguin Random House, 2021 with Bobbie Nelson and David Ritz
* *Sister, Brother, Family: An American Childhood in Music*, Penguin Random House, 2021 with Bobbie Nelson and Chris Barton
* *Me And Paul: Untold Stories of a Fabled Friendship*, HarperCollins, 2022 with David Ritz
* *Energy Follows Thought: The Stories Behind My Songs*, HarperCollins, 2023 with Mickey Raphael and David Ritz
